title: Game Feel & Juice: Tweening, Screen Shake & VFX
html:
Why the same game can feel dead or alive

Two games can have identical rules and one feels like a toy while the other feels amazing. The difference is game feel (often called juice): the layer of secondary motion, timing tricks, and feedback that confirms every player action with sight, sound, and motion. None of it changes the rules — it changes how the rules feel. In this lesson we build a small, reusable juice toolkit (tweens, screen shake, hit-stop, flashes, particles, and audio blips) and apply it as a polish pass over a game you already built earlier in this track.

Easing & a tiny tween engine

Linear motion looks robotic. Real motion accelerates and decelerates. Easing functions remap a normalized time t in [0,1] to an eased value, also in [0,1] (usually). You lerp between a start and end value using the eased t.

ease.js
export const Ease = { linear: t => t, inQuad: t => t * t, outQuad: t => t * (2 - t), inOutQuad: t => t < .5 ? 2*t*t : -1 + (4 - 2*t)*t, outCubic: t => { const p = t - 1; return p*p*p + 1; }, // overshoot & settle — great for UI pop-in outBack: t => { const s = 1.70158, p = t - 1; return p*p*((s+1)*p + s) + 1; }, // bouncy spring outElastic: t => t === 0 || t === 1 ? t : Math.pow(2, -10*t) * Math.sin((t - .075) * (2*Math.PI) / .3) + 1 };

Now a tween manager. A tween owns an object, a key, a target, a duration, and an easing function. Each frame it advances by dt and writes the interpolated value back. Tweens self-remove when finished and can fire an onDone callback.

tween.js
import { Ease } from './ease.js'; export class Tweens { constructor() { this.list = []; } to(obj, key, target, dur, ease = Ease.outCubic, onDone) { this.list.push({ obj, key, from: obj[key], target, dur, t: 0, ease, onDone }); } update(dt) { for (let i = this.list.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { const w = this.list[i]; w.t = Math.min(1, w.t + dt / w.dur); const k = w.ease(w.t); w.obj[w.key] = w.from + (w.target - w.from) * k; if (w.t >= 1) { this.list.splice(i, 1); w.onDone && w.onDone(); } } } }

Use it everywhere: tweens.to(coin, 'scale', 1, 0.25, Ease.outBack) for a pop-in, or animate a menu panel’s x onto screen. Note the from snapshot is captured at creation time, so re-issuing a tween mid-flight starts from the current value — exactly what you want.

Screen shake the right way

Bad shake jitters forever and nauseates players. Good shake is a short, decaying burst of random offset applied to the camera before you render the world. Store a trauma value; square it so small hits are subtle and big hits punch; decay it every frame.

shake.js
export class Shake { constructor() { this.trauma = 0; this.ox = 0; this.oy = 0; } add(amount) { this.trauma = Math.min(1, this.trauma + amount); } update(dt) { const s = this.trauma * this.trauma; // quadratic feel const max = 14; this.ox = (Math.random() * 2 - 1) * max * s; this.oy = (Math.random() * 2 - 1) * max * s; this.trauma = Math.max(0, this.trauma - dt * 1.8); } } // render: ctx.save(); ctx.translate(shake.ox, shake.oy); drawWorld(); ctx.restore();
Tip.
Add a tiny random rotation ((Math.random()*2-1)*0.03*s) on top of translation. The eye reads rotation as far more violent than pure translation, so a little goes a long way.

Hit-stop: the most underrated trick

Hit-stop (a.k.a. freeze-frame) briefly pauses or slows simulation at the moment of impact, selling weight. Implement it as a global time-scale plus a short timer. When the timer is active, scale dt down (or to zero) for gameplay updates — but keep updating juice systems so shake and flashes still animate.

hitstop.js
const juice = { freeze: 0, scale: 1 }; function hitStop(seconds, slow = 0) { juice.freeze = seconds; juice.scale = slow; } function update(dt) { shake.update(dt); tweens.update(dt); particles.update(dt); // juice runs at real time let g = dt; if (juice.freeze > 0) { juice.freeze -= dt; g = dt * juice.scale; } world.update(g); // gameplay runs at scaled time } // on enemy death: hitStop(0.06); shake.add(0.5);

Flashes, particles & audio feedback

A hit flash tints a sprite white for a few frames using globalCompositeOperation = 'lighter' or by drawing a white silhouette. A small particle burst on impact (reuse the pool from the twin-stick lesson) reads as debris or sparks. And cheap audio via Web Audio is instant feedback — synthesize a blip with an oscillator, no asset files needed.

sfx.js
const ac = new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)(); export function blip(freq = 440, dur = 0.08, type = 'square') { const o = ac.createOscillator(), g = ac.createGain(); o.type = type; o.frequency.value = freq; o.connect(g); g.connect(ac.destination); const t = ac.currentTime; g.gain.setValueAtTime(0.2, t); g.gain.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(0.0001, t + dur); // quick decay = punchy o.start(t); o.stop(t + dur); } // resume on first user gesture: window.addEventListener('pointerdown',()=>ac.resume(),{once:true});
The juice law.
Every meaningful player action deserves at least one piece of feedback across two senses. A shot should flash AND click. A hit should shake AND crunch. Feedback that confirms cause-and-effect is what makes a game feel responsive.
Don’t over-juice.
Hit-stop on every frame, constant shake, and screen-filling particles cause motion sickness and hide the game. Reserve big juice for big moments (kills, level-clear) and keep ambient actions subtle. Always offer a “reduce screen shake” / “reduce motion” toggle.

Anticipation & follow-through

Animators know movement reads better with a wind-up before and an overshoot after. In code: scale a sprite to 0.85 for a few frames before a jump (squash), then tween to 1.15 and back to 1 on landing (stretch) with Ease.outBack. Combined with a tiny landing shake and a dust particle, a plain jump suddenly feels alive.

🎮 Build This: Game feel / polish — Juice Pass

This is a polish pass that EXTENDS a prior game (pick any one you already built — Breakout, the twin-stick shooter, or the platformer all work great). You add no new rules, only feel.

  1. Drop in ease.js, tween.js, shake.js, and sfx.js as reusable modules.
  2. Wrap your world render in ctx.translate(shake.ox, shake.oy) and call shake.update(dt) each frame.
  3. On every impact (brick break, enemy hit, jump-land), call shake.add(), spawn a particle burst, and play a blip() at a pitch that varies with the event.
  4. Add a global hit-stop: freeze gameplay ~60ms on a kill while juice systems keep running.
  5. Tween UI: pop the score with Ease.outBack when it changes; squash-and-stretch the player on jump or dash.
  6. Add a settings toggle that scales shake/particles to zero for accessibility, and confirm the game is still fully playable with juice off.

title: Performance Profiling, Saving & Multiplayer Basics
html:
From “it works” to “it ships”

A game that runs at 60 fps on your dev machine can crawl on a phone, lose progress on refresh, and never reach a second player. This lesson covers the three engineering disciplines that turn a prototype into a real product: profiling & optimization, saving state, and basic online multiplayer with WebSockets. This is a concept lesson — the techniques apply to every game in the track.

Measure first: the DevTools Performance panel

Never optimize on a hunch. Open DevTools, go to the Performance tab, record a few seconds of gameplay, and look at the flame chart. You are hunting for three things: long scripting bars (your update logic), long rendering/painting bars (canvas draws), and frequent GC sawtooth spikes in the memory timeline (garbage collection pauses). The performance.now() timer and console.time let you bracket suspicious code precisely.

profile.js
const t0 = performance.now(); world.update(dt); const t1 = performance.now(); physics.step(dt); const t2 = performance.now(); render(); const t3 = performance.now(); // budget is 16.67ms at 60fps — see where it goes hud.debug = `upd ${(t1-t0).toFixed(2)} phys ${(t2-t1).toFixed(2)} draw ${(t3-t2).toFixed(2)}`;
The 16.67 ms budget.
60 fps means each frame must finish in 1000/60 ≈ 16.67ms — including update, physics, render, AND the browser’s own compositing. If your code uses 14ms you have almost no headroom. Aim to keep your work under ~8–10ms.

Killing garbage-collection stutter

The number-one cause of micro-stutter in JS games is allocation in the hot loop. Every {x,y} literal, every arr.map(), every new vector object creates garbage that the GC must later collect, freezing the frame. The fixes: reuse objects, pool entities, and avoid array methods that allocate.

nogc.js
// BAD: allocates a new object every frame, every entity function update(e) { e.vel = { x: e.vel.x, y: e.vel.y + g }; } // GOOD: mutate in place — zero allocation function update(e) { e.vy += g; } // BAD in hot loop: creates intermediate arrays each frame bullets.filter(b => b.alive).forEach(draw); // GOOD: plain for-loop, no closures, no temp arrays for (let i = 0; i < bullets.length; i++) { const b = bullets[i]; if (b.alive) draw(b); }

Render hot-path optimization

Canvas drawing is often the real bottleneck. Key wins: batch state changes (set fillStyle once, draw many), cull off-screen entities, pre-render static or expensive art to an offscreen canvas once and blit it, and snap drawing coordinates to integers (sub-pixel positions force the browser to anti-alias, which is slower and blurrier).

render-opt.js
// Pre-render an expensive tile once into an offscreen canvas const off = document.createElement('canvas'); off.width = off.height = 32; const octx = off.getContext('2d'); drawFancyTile(octx); // runs ONCE function render() { for (let i = 0; i < tiles.length; i++) { const t = tiles[i]; if (!onScreen(t, cam)) continue; // cull ctx.drawImage(off, (t.x - cam.x) | 0, (t.y - cam.y) | 0); // |0 snaps to int, blits cached pixels } }
Tip.
Run Chrome’s Rendering tab with “Paint flashing” on to see exactly which regions repaint each frame, and “Frame Rendering Stats” for a live fps/GPU overlay. If everything flashes every frame in a mostly-static scene, you’re over-drawing.

Saving & loading with localStorage

Persisting progress is just serializing a plain-data snapshot to JSON and storing it under a versioned key. Keep a schema version so future updates can migrate old saves instead of crashing on them. Never store live class instances — store the data you need to reconstruct them.

save.js
const KEY = 'mygame.save', VERSION = 2; export function save(state) { const data = { v: VERSION, level: state.level, hp: state.hp, coins: state.coins, items: state.items }; localStorage.setItem(KEY, JSON.stringify(data)); } export function load() { const raw = localStorage.getItem(KEY); if (!raw) return null; let d; try { d = JSON.parse(raw); } catch { return null; } // corrupt save — ignore if (d.v < VERSION) d = migrate(d); // upgrade old schema return d; } function migrate(d) { if (d.v === 1) { d.items = []; d.v = 2; } return d; }
localStorage is synchronous & small.
Writing blocks the main thread, so do NOT save every frame — save on level change, on pause, and via the visibilitychange event. The quota is only ~5MB per origin, and the data is plain text the player can edit, so never trust it for anti-cheat.

Basic online multiplayer with WebSockets

WebSockets give you a persistent, low-latency, bidirectional channel — ideal for real-time games. The browser opens a socket; a small server relays messages between players. Start with the simplest possible model: each client sends its own input/state, the server broadcasts to everyone, and each client renders the others.

net-client.js
const ws = new WebSocket('wss://your-server.example/play'); ws.onopen = () => console.log('connected'); ws.onmessage = (ev) => { const msg = JSON.parse(ev.data); if (msg.t === 'state') remote[msg.id] = msg; // other players }; // send our state at a fixed network tick, NOT every frame setInterval(() => { if (ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) ws.send(JSON.stringify({ t: 'state', x: player.x, y: player.y, a: player.angle })); }, 50); // 20 Hz
server.js (Node + ws)
import { WebSocketServer } from 'ws'; const wss = new WebSocketServer({ port: 8080 }); let nextId = 1; wss.on('connection', (sock) => { const id = nextId++; sock.on('message', (buf) => { const msg = JSON.parse(buf); msg.id = id; const out = JSON.stringify(msg); wss.clients.forEach(c => { if (c !== sock && c.readyState === 1) c.send(out); }); // relay to others }); });
Smooth the network.
20 Hz updates look jerky if drawn raw. Interpolate remote players toward their latest received position each frame (lerp by ~0.2), and buffer a frame or two so a dropped packet doesn’t freeze them. This hides jitter without needing prediction.
Authoritative vs. trusting.
A relay server (above) trusts every client — fine for co-op or friendly games. For competitive play, the server must own the simulation and validate inputs, or players will cheat by sending fake positions. Start with a relay, graduate to authoritative when stakes rise.
🎮 Build This: Optimization / Online — Co-op Lobby

This EXTENDS a prior game (the top-down Zelda-like adventure or the twin-stick shooter are ideal) by profiling it and adding save + co-op. It does not start a new genre.

  1. Record a Performance profile during heavy gameplay; identify your single worst scripting and rendering cost.
  2. Eliminate hot-loop allocations: convert closures/maps to plain for-loops and mutate vectors in place. Re-profile to confirm GC sawtooth shrinks.
  3. Cull off-screen entities and pre-render one expensive sprite to an offscreen canvas; snap draws to integers.
  4. Add versioned save()/load() via localStorage; persist on level change and visibilitychange, with a migration path.
  5. Stand up the Node ws relay server and connect two browser tabs; render the remote player as a second avatar.
  6. Send state at 20 Hz and interpolate the remote player each frame so movement looks smooth.

title: Capstone: Build, Polish & Deploy Your Own Game
html:
Everything you’ve built, in one game

You’ve hand-coded a game loop, an ECS, an input manager, vectors, AABB and circle collision, tilemaps, cameras, particles, raycasting, pseudo-3D roads, pathfinding, physics, audio, juice, profiling, saving, and networking — with no engine. The capstone is where you stop following recipes and ship something that is yours. This lesson is the playbook: scope it, assemble the engine, polish it, and deploy it to the open web so anyone can play.

Scope like a professional

The number-one reason capstones fail is over-scoping. A finished tiny game beats an unfinished epic every time. Define a single-sentence pitch, one core loop, and a minimum viable game (MVG) you could ship this week — then add polish only after the MVG is fully playable start-to-finish.

The vertical-slice rule.
Build one complete level/round that is genuinely fun before you build the second. If a single slice isn’t fun, ten copies of it won’t be either. Get the core loop feeling great first; content comes after.
ScopePitchEngine pieces reused
FPSSurvive waves in a raycast mazeraycasting, sprites, pooling, juice
RacerBeat-the-clock OutRun circuitpseudo-3D road, input, save (best lap)
RoguelikeDescend a procedural dungeonprocgen, FOV, turn combat, save
Tower defenseHold the line for 10 wavesA* pathfinding, waves, particles, juice

Assemble the engine cleanly

Pull your reusable modules into a single engine/ folder and your game-specific code into game/. The engine should never import from the game — dependencies point one way. A clean main.js wires them together: create the canvas, the loop, the input manager, the state machine, and start in a menu state.

main.js
import { Loop } from './engine/loop.js'; import { Input } from './engine/input.js'; import { StateMachine } from './engine/state.js'; import { MenuState, PlayState, GameOverState } from './game/states.js'; const canvas = document.getElementById('game'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false; const input = new Input(canvas); const sm = new StateMachine({ menu: new MenuState(), play: new PlayState(), over: new GameOverState() }); sm.set('menu', { input, ctx, canvas }); const loop = new Loop( dt => { input.update(); sm.update(dt); }, () => sm.render(ctx) ); loop.start();

The polish checklist

Polish is what separates a class project from something strangers enjoy. Walk this list before you call it done:

  • First-run clarity — a title screen, a one-line how-to-play, and controls shown on screen.
  • Game feel — apply the juice pass: tweened UI, screen shake, hit-stop, particles, audio blips.
  • Win/lose loop — the player can always get back to playing in one click; show score and a restart.
  • Persistence — save high score / progress with versioned localStorage.
  • Performance — profile on a low-end device; hold 60 fps; no GC stutter.
  • Accessibility — remappable keys, a reduce-motion toggle, pause on tab blur.
  • Responsiveness — the canvas scales to the viewport and works on touch.
resize.js
const BASE_W = 960, BASE_H = 540; // fixed internal resolution function fit() { const scale = Math.min(innerWidth / BASE_W, innerHeight / BASE_H); canvas.style.width = (BASE_W * scale) + 'px'; canvas.style.height = (BASE_H * scale) + 'px'; } canvas.width = BASE_W; canvas.height = BASE_H; // crisp, fixed buffer addEventListener('resize', fit); fit(); document.addEventListener('visibilitychange', () => { if (document.hidden) loop.pause(); });
Tip.
Keep a fixed internal resolution and scale only with CSS (above). Your game logic never deals with the real pixel size of the screen, so collision, cameras, and aiming stay simple and consistent across every device.

Bundle for production

You can ship raw ES modules directly — modern browsers load them natively. For a faster, single-file build, a zero-config bundler like Vite tree-shakes and minifies everything into a tiny dist/.

terminal
# scaffold & build with Vite npm create vite@latest my-game -- --template vanilla cd my-game # drop your engine/ game/ main.js in, point index.html at main.js npm install npm run build # outputs minified dist/ npm run preview # test the production build locally
ES modules need a server.
Opening index.html via file:// breaks module imports (CORS) and image/audio loads. Always test through a local server (npm run dev, or npx serve). The same rule is why your built game must be hosted, not emailed as a file.

Deploy to the web

A built canvas game is just static files — HTML, JS, and assets — so any static host works for free. Cloudflare Pages, Netlify, GitHub Pages, and Vercel all deploy in minutes. Connect your repo (or drag the dist/ folder), set the build command to npm run build and the output dir to dist, and you get a public URL with HTTPS automatically.

deploy (Cloudflare Pages via Wrangler)
npm run build npx wrangler pages deploy dist --project-name my-game # → prints a live https://my-game.pages.dev URL
Tip.
HTTPS is not optional — the Gamepad API, fullscreen, pointer lock (great for the FPS), and resumed AudioContext all behave best on a secure origin. Every host above gives you free HTTPS, so deploy early and test on a real phone via the live URL.

Ship it, then iterate

Done is a feature. Get a playable build in front of real people, watch where they get confused or bored, and fix the top one or two issues — that loop will teach you more than any tutorial. Then add the second level, the leaderboard, the multiplayer mode. You now own the whole stack, from the pixel to the deploy.

🎮 Build This: Capstone — Your Original Game

This is the CAPSTONE: it EXTENDS the entire track into one original game in the genre you choose. You are not starting a single new genre — you are combining the full hand-coded engine you built across Part 06 into a finished, deployed product.

  1. Write a one-sentence pitch and define the MVG — the smallest version that is fully playable start to finish.
  2. Assemble your reusable modules into engine/ and game code into game/, wired by a clean main.js with a state machine (menu → play → game over).
  3. Build one complete, genuinely fun vertical slice before adding more content.
  4. Run the polish checklist: title screen, juice pass, win/lose loop, versioned save, profiled 60 fps, reduce-motion toggle, responsive scaling.
  5. Bundle with Vite (npm run build) and verify the production build via npm run preview.
  6. Deploy the dist/ folder to a static host (Cloudflare Pages / Netlify / GitHub Pages), test on a real phone via the HTTPS URL, then share it and gather feedback.
